perturbation.original_text: Arizona agrees to assign and hereby assigns its entire right, title and interest in and to the Arizona Assigned IP to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Arizona agrees to assign its right, title and interest in and to the Arizona Assigned IP to the Company only in case of successful financial performance in the specified financial period.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates an outright assignment of intellectual property (IP). The modified text introduces a condition of 'successful financial performance' before the assignment takes effect. This change contradicts the concept of assignment, which generally implies an immediate transfer of rights. By making it conditional, the contract deviates from the established legal understanding of IP assignment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces a condition (successful financial performance) precedent to the assignment of IP rights. This conditionality creates ambiguity and potential conflict with the statute, as it implies that the assignment is not absolute or immediate upon execution of the agreement, contradicting the ownership rights transfer upon assignment described in the statute. The added condition could be interpreted as creating a security interest rather than an outright assignment, further complicating the matter under intellectual property law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates an outright assignment of intellectual property, whereas the modified text introduces a condition of 'successful financial performance' before the assignment takes effect. The modified text contradicts the concept of assignment, which implies an immediate transfer of rights. The law snippets state that applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing, which implies an immediate transfer. Making the assignment conditional deviates from the established legal understanding of IP assignment.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-free, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a limited, non- exclusive, royalty-based, non-sublicensable (except as set forth in Section 7.1), non-assignable license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Trademarks for the Arizona Trademark License Term for use in the Company Field throughout the world only in the form and manner that such Arizona Licensed Trademarks are used in the Business as of the Closing, provided that the Company shall use commercially reasonable efforts to present the Arizona Licensed Trademarks in the form set forth on Schedule 6.1.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'royalty-free' with 'royalty-based'. This introduces a contradiction because the agreement does not specify the royalty rate or payment terms, rendering the license terms vague and potentially unenforceable. Trademark licenses must have clearly defined terms to be valid.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the license from 'royalty-free' to 'royalty-based' without specifying the royalty rate or payment terms, the agreement creates uncertainty regarding the financial obligations of the licensee. This vagueness can lead to disputes about infringement remedies and whether the licensee is operating within the scope of the license, potentially conflicting with the statute's provisions regarding infringement liability and remedies.

Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. The United States, all agencies and instrume
perturbation.justification: The change from 'royalty-free' to 'royalty-based' without specifying any royalty rate or payment terms introduces a significant ambiguity regarding the financial obligations of the licensee. While the provided law snippets (15 USC 1114) primarily address remedies for infringement, they underscore the necessity for clarity and consent in trademark usage agreements. By failing to define the royalty terms, the modified agreement creates uncertainty about whether the licensee is operating within the bounds of the agreement, potentially leading to disputes about infringement. This directly contradicts the principles of trademark law, which require clear and unambiguous licensing terms to ensure that both parties understand their rights and obligations, making this agreement potentially unenforceable due to vagueness and creating a clear legal contradiction under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party (the "Indemnifying Party") agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other Party and its Affiliates and their respective employees, 9 directors, officers, agents and successors (collectively, the "Indemnified Parties") from and against any and all losses (including all costs, liabilities (including present and future damages), claims and expenses) incurred or suffered by any of the Indemnified Parties, to the extent arising out of, relating to or resulting from (a) a breach by the Indemnifying Party of this Agreement; or (b) any gross negligence or willful misconduct of the Indemnifying Party in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party (the "Indemnifying Party") agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other Party and its Affiliates and their respective employees, 9 directors, officers, agents and successors (collectively, the "Indemnified Parties") from and against any and all losses (including all costs, liabilities (including present and future damages), claims and expenses) incurred or suffered by any of the Indemnified Parties, to the extent arising out of, relating to or resulting from (a) a breach by the Indemnifying Party of this Agreement; or (b) any negligence of the Indemnifying Party in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause limits indemnification to instances of 'gross negligence or willful misconduct.' The modified clause expands it to 'any negligence.' This is problematic because ordinary negligence is a lower standard of fault, and broadly indemnifying for any negligence may be against public policy or state laws that limit the scope of indemnification agreements. Many states have laws that prohibit or limit indemnification for a party's own negligence, especially in certain contexts like construction contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 2782 - Construction contracts; invalidity of provisions
perturbation.law_explanation: This change from 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' to 'any negligence' broadens the scope of indemnification. Under California Civil Code § 2782, provisions in construction contracts that purport to indemnify the promisee against liability for their own negligence are generally void and unenforceable. While this IP agreement isn't a construction contract, many states have similar public policy considerations limiting indemnification for one's own negligence. This modified clause could be deemed unenforceable to the extent it requires a party to indemnify another for the other party's sole negligence, violating public policy or similar statutory restrictions on indemnification agreements.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2782. (a) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, 2782.5, and 2782.6, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affecting any construction contract and that purport to indemnify the promisee against liability for damages for death or bodily injury to persons, injury to property, or any other loss, damage or expense arising from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee or the promisee’s agents, servants, or independent contractors who are directly responsible to the promisee, or for defects in design furnished by those persons, are against public policy and are void and unenforceable; provided, however, that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers’ compensation, or agreement issued by an admitted insurer as defined by the Insurance Code. (b) (1) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, and 2782.5, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affectin
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the scope of indemnification from 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' to 'any negligence,' potentially violating public policy or state laws limiting indemnification for one's own negligence. California Civil Code § 2782(a), as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that provisions in construction contracts that indemnify the promisee against liability for damages arising from their sole negligence are against public policy and unenforceable. While this is an IP agreement and not a construction contract, the Law Explanation points out that many states have similar public policy considerations that could render the modified clause unenforceable to the extent it requires a party to indemnify another for the other party's sole negligence.
